coredata.dc:title: A facile label-free colorimetric method for highly sensitive glutathione detection by using manganese dioxide nanosheets
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Zhong-Ming
Cai, Qi-Yong
Ding, De-Cai
Ge, Jia
Hu, Ya-Lei
Yang, Jie
Zhang, Lin
Li, Zhao-Hui
coredata.dc:description: Glutathione (GSH) provides essential functions towards metabolism process in biological systems and is related to many diseases such as human immunodeficiency virus, Parkinson, cardiovascular diseases, etc. In this work, a very simple, fast and label-free colorimetric method for highly sensitive GSH quantification is proposed. This strategy is based on the color change of MnO2 nanosheets solution from yellowish-brown to colorless. Without GSH, MnO2 nanosheets exhibit a strong ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption peak at 376nm with the color showing as yellowish-brown. Once GSH is introduced, MnO2 nanosheets are reduced to Mn2+ ions rapidly, which leads to a yellowish-brown color fading and a remarkable decrease of the absorbance at 376nm. Based on the distinct color change, a method for highly sensitive and selective detection of GSH was realized with bare eyes and UV–vis spectroscopy, respectively. This method has a wide dynamic range from 0.5μM to 100μΜ and shows a good linear relationship from 0.5μM to 10μΜ with a detection limit of 0.1μM. More importantly, this procedure is very simple and the whole assay can be accomplished quickly within 5min. Additionally, this developed assay has also been successfully applied to the detection of GSH in biological fluids with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glutathione detection
MnO2 nanosheets
UV–vis
Label-free

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of GSH in human serum
Synthesis and characterization of MnO2 nanosheets
Sensing of GSH by using MnO2 nanosheets as colorimetric probes
Selectivity of this MnO2 nanosheets-based GSH assay

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21205108 , 21505122 , J1210060 ), the Startup Research Fund of Zhengzhou University ( 1511316004 ) and the Outstanding Young Talent Research Fund of Zhengzhou University ( 1421316038 , 1521316003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of glutathione detection by using MnO2 nanosheets as colorimetric probe.

(A) Digital images of MnCl2 solution (left), TMA·OH/H2O2 solution (middle), and MnO2 nanosheets solution (right). (B) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnCl2 (red line), TMA·OH/H2O2 (green line), and MnO2 nanosheets (black line), respectively. (C) TEM image of MnO2 nanosheets. (D) The X-ray diffraction pattern of MnO2 nanosheets. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets solution in the absence of GSH (black line) and in the presence of GSH with concentrations of 50μM (green line) and 150μM (red line). Inset shows the corresponding photograph of MnO2 nanosheets solution. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The absorption spectra of MnO2 nanosheets solution with increasing concentration of GSH in the range of 0–100μM (From top to bottom: 0μM, 0.5μM, 1μM, 3μM, 5μM, 7μM, 9μM, 10μM, 20μM, 30μM, 40μM, 50μM, 60μM, 70μM, 80μM, 90μM and 100μM). (B) Linear relationship between (A0 −A)/A0 and GSH concentration, where A0 represents the absorbance of MnO2 nanosheets solution at 376nm without GSH, and A represents the absorbance of MnO2 nanosheets solution at 376nm with different GSH concentration. (C) Digital images of MnO2 nanosheets solution with different concentration of GSH (From left to right: 0μΜ, 20μΜ, 50μΜ, 80μΜ, 100μΜ, 120μΜ, and 150μΜ).

Relative absorption intensity of MnO2 nanosheets solution in the presence of various electrolytes and biomolecules (1mM of each, BSA 1mg/mL), where A0 represents the absorption intensity of MnO2 nanosheets solution at 376nm and A represents the absorption intensity after adding different interferences and 100μM GSH.
Analytical results of the determination of GSH in human serum sample using MnO2 nanosheets-based colorimetric method and a commercial GSH assay kit as controlled trial.

head.ce:title: A facile label-free colorimetric method for highly sensitive glutathione detection by using manganese dioxide nanosheets

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile label-free colorimetric probe for highly sensitive glutathione quantification by using manganese dioxide nanosheets.

Glutathione (GSH) provides essential functions towards metabolism process in biological systems and is related to many diseases such as human immunodeficiency virus, Parkinson, cardiovascular diseases, etc. In this work, a very simple, fast and label-free colorimetric method for highly sensitive GSH quantification is proposed. This strategy is based on the color change of MnO2 nanosheets solution from yellowish-brown to colorless. Without GSH, MnO2 nanosheets exhibit a strong ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption peak at 376nm with the color showing as yellowish-brown. Once GSH is introduced, MnO2 nanosheets are reduced to Mn2+ ions rapidly, which leads to a yellowish-brown color fading and a remarkable decrease of the absorbance at 376nm. Based on the distinct color change, a method for highly sensitive and selective detection of GSH was realized with bare eyes and UV–vis spectroscopy, respectively. This method has a wide dynamic range from 0.5μM to 100μΜ and shows a good linear relationship from 0.5μM to 10μΜ with a detection limit of 0.1μM. More importantly, this procedure is very simple and the whole assay can be accomplished quickly within 5min. Additionally, this developed assay has also been successfully applied to the detection of GSH in biological fluids with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) is an important predominant non-protein thiol widely distributed in mammalian and eukaryotic cells. GSH serves several essential functions in biological systems, such as reduces intracellular free radicals, maintains the metabolism procedure, and prevents organism from toxins [1–6]. The abnormal level of GSH has been reported to be closely related to aging and many human diseases, such as canner, liver damage, heart problems, Parkinson and Alzheimer, etc [7–9]. Accordingly, sensitive detection of GSH with high simplicity is of sustained interest to satisfy clinical and medical requirements. To date, many approaches have been developed for the detection of GSH including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10–12], electrochemical analysis [13–15], capillary electrophoresis [16], fluorescence spectroscopy [17–21], magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [22] and colorimetry [23]. Among them, colorimetric method offers promising advantages due to its high sensitivity, simplicity and low cost. Moreover, the detection results of colorimetric method can be easily readout with bare eyes through a visual color change in reaction media.

Over the past several years, a number of colorimetric sensing mechanisms have been employed for GSH detection. The gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)-based colorimetric sensor has been developed for GSH detection based on the color changing of AuNPs in the turnover process of dispersion to aggregation state [24]. Liu et al. [25] reported a simple colorimetric method to determine GSH by utilizing BSA–MnO2 nanoparticles as mimic. BSA-MnO2 nanoparticles possess oxidase-like activity that can catalyze the oxidization of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), and the introduce of GSH will cause reduction of oxidized TMB along with a visual color variation. Recently, Ni et al. [23] established an assay for quantitative detection of GSH based on the color change of TMB that was oxidized by silver ion. Although the reported colorimetric methods have shown sensitive and rapid properties, these strategies still suffer from some disadvantages such as time-consuming laborious synthesis to obtain probes and participation of high-cost noble metals (gold, silver). Additionally, the activity of mimic materials is highly dependent on some environmental conditions such as salt concentration and incubation time, which may bring the effect of accuracy reduce. Thus, there is still an increasing demand for developing simple, low-cost and rapid GSH assay in biological and clinical occasions.

Recently, our group [21] has developed a fluorescence method to detect GSH by using carbon dots–MnO2 nanocomposites. In this assay, fluorescence of carbon dots can be quenched by MnO2 nanosheets and will be recovered quickly when GSH is introduced, which causes the rapid reduction of MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+. Afterwards, we realized that MnO2 nanosheets could also be used as colorimetric probes for the determination of GSH without any other nanomaterials. Very simply, detection of GSH can be accomplished through directly visualizing the color variation of MnO2 nanosheets by bare eyes or UV–vis spectroscopy. Herein in this work, we report a simple, label-free and low-cost method for GSH detection using MnO2 nanosheets as colorimetric probe. Scheme 1 illustrates the mechanism of this proposed method for GSH detection. In the presence of GSH, MnO2 nanosheets will be reduced to Mn2+ ions with yellowish-brown color fading of the solution. As a result, the UV–vis absorption of MnO2 nanosheets solution is dependent upon the concentration of GSH, which allows its colorimetric detection. This assay is very fast and the whole process can be accomplished in 5min with a low detection limit of 0.1μM. More importantly, the analytical potential of this proposed assay in practical applications was also demonstrated successfully for analyzing GSH in human serum samples.

Glutathione (reduced form) and Tetramethylammonium hydroxide were obtained from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). l-Glycine (Gly), l-leucine (Leu), l-isoleucine (Ile), l-proline (Pro), l-serine (Ser), l-valine (Val), glucose, sucrose and BSA were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich reagent Co. Ltd. (St. Louis, MO, USA). NaCl, KCl, Na2SO4, MnCl2·4H2O (99.99%), H2O2 (ω=30%) and other salts were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). GSH assay kit was purchased from Beyotime biological technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All of these reagents were used as received without any further purification. All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water generated by a Millipore Milli-Q water purification system (Billerica, MA, USA) with an electric resistance ≥18.2MΩ.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on TU-1810 ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometer (Beijing Persee Co., Ltd., China). Morphology images of MnO2 nanosheets were photographed by a transmission electron microscope (FEI-Tecnai G2, USA) at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The crystal form characterization was measured with X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical, Netherlands).

MnO2 nanosheets were prepared according to the previously reported method [26] with minor modification. In a typical synthesis process, 8mL tetramethylammonium (TMA·OH, 1.0M) and 1.5mL H2O2 were mixed and then diluted to 15mL with ultrapure water. After that, the solution was mixed with 0.415g MnCl2·4H2O within 30s. The mixture was stirred vigorously overnight and MnO2 nanosheets were obtained from the suspension by centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 10min, followed by washing three times with methanol. Then, MnO2 nanosheets were washed three times with deionized water. To obtain homogeneous MnO2 nanosheets solution, the as-prepared MnO2 samples were further treated by a SCIENTZ-IID ultrasonic homogenizer (Ningbo Scientz Biotechnology Co., Ltd, China).

In a typical GSH assay, 600μL MnO2 nanosheets solution (35μg/mL) was added into a centrifuge tube containing 2.37mL ultrapure water, and then mixed with 30μL GSH with the concentration in a range of 0–10mM. After 5min, UV–vis absorption spectra were measured by spectrophotometer. Meanwhile, photographs were captured by a digital camera (Canon, EOS 6D, Japan).

Selectivity study was performed as following: 300μL interfering species including electrolytes (NaCl, KCl, Na2SO4, MnCl2)(10mM), glucose (10mM), sucrose (10mM), BSA (10mg/mL), PBS (10mM), or amino acid (Ser, Arg, Val, Ile, His, Pro, Gly, Leu)(10mM) were mixed with 2.1mL ultrapure water, respectively. Then 600μL MnO2 nanosheets were added into the solution and UV–vis absorbance change was monitored by a spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, human serum samples were ultra-filtered to remove some protein and enzyme and the filtrate was diluted 200 times to make sure the concentration of GSH in human serum sample falls into the detection range. Then 30μL GSH with different concentrations including 200μM, 400μM and 600μM was mixed with 2.37mL diluted human serum. After that, 600μL MnO2 nanosheets were added and the absorption signals were recorded by a spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: MnO2 nanosheets were characterized by UV–vis spectroscopy, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 1 A, after TMA·OH, MnCl2·4H2O and H2O2 were mixed together, the color of the solution turned from clear to dark brown, which is due to the formation of MnO2 nanosheets. When being diluted to a certain concentration, the solution turns to yellowish-brown. Fig. 1B demonstrates that the obtained MnO2 nanosheets have a wide UV–vis absorption spectrum from 240nm to 700nm with a strong absorption peak centered at 376nm, while MnCl2 solution has no UV–vis spectrum absorption and TMA·OH/H2O2 only has an absorption range less than 350nm. To further verify the product, morphology of MnO2 nanosheets were characterized by TEM. TEM image (Fig. 1C) shows that the prepared MnO2 nanosheets exhibit a typical two-dimensional and ultrathin lamellar morphology. Fragments were also observed in small amounts. Meanwhile, the nanosheets with occasional folds and wrinkles have been observed, and the average lateral dimension was estimated as submicrometer. In the XRD pattern (Fig. 1D), the two diffraction peaks are located at about 36° and 65°, which are indexed as (100) and (110) for a hexagonal two-dimensional unit cell. The intense reflections (00l, l:1, 2, 3) are associated with the preferred orientation, in which two well-defined XRD signal peaks appearing at about 9.5° and 18° are assigned to the (001) and (002) reflection of birnessite-type MnO2, indicating that the as-prepared nanosheet has a layered structure as well [26]. All the results demonstrate the successful preparation of MnO2 nanosheets. Furthermore, the stability of these MnO2 nanosheets was investigated and it turned out that there was no precipitate observed and no obvious absorption changed even after one month, which indicated the excellent stability of these MnO2 nanosheets and provided a strong support for the following experiments.

In order to investigate the feasibility of this colorimetric assay, photograph images and corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra of MnO2 nanosheets solution were recorded in the absence and presence of GSH, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 , MnO2 nanosheets solution shows yellowish-brown color with a strong absorption peak centered at 376nm. And right after 50μM GSH is introduced, the color of MnO2 nanosheets solution fades to light brown with the absorption intensity thoroughly decreased. As the concentration of GSH is increased to 150μM, the solution turns to colorless and the characteristic absorption band of MnO2 nanosheets at 376nm almost disappears, which is due to the reduction of MnO2 nanosheets to form Mn2+ ions. The reaction of MnO2 to Mn2+can be shown as the follow equation [18]:
MnO2
+
2GSH
+
2H+ →Mn2+ +GSSG+2H2O
The results demonstrate that the colorimetric method based on MnO2 nanosheets could be successfully used to detect the concentration of GSH.

To obtain reliable result and expand the application of this nanosensor, we conducted the detection process at different temperature. The result is shown in Fig. S1 and no obvious absorbance change can be observed, indicating that temperature has little influence on the detection result. Therefore, room temperature was chosen as the optimal condition for the following experiment to make this assay more practical. Meanwhile, the influence of pH value was investigated for this assay and the result is shown in Fig. S2. Accordingly, MnO2 nanosheets are unstable when the pH value is lower than 5.2 whereas with the pH of 6.8, 7.4, and 8.2, the corresponding absorbance is similar with that in ultrapure water. Thus for the flexibility purpose, ultrapure water was used throughout the whole experiment. In order to investigate the effect of reaction time on the signal output of the proposed assay, UV–vis absorption intensity of MnO2 nanosheets solution at 376nm in the presence of GSH was studied as shown in Fig. S3. When GSH is added into MnO2 nanosheets solution, the absorbance signal decreases rapidly with the increasing of incubation time and then reaches to a stable stage after 5min. Thus, 5min was chosen as the appropriate time in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of GSH by using MnO2 nanosheets as colorimetric probes
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the performance of this sensing strategy, the absorbance of MnO2 nanosheets solution at 376nm was monitored as a function of GSH concentration under the optimal experimental conditions. Fig. 3 A shows the absorption spectra of MnO2 nanosheets solution with a broad GSH concentration range from 0 to 100μM. Furthermore, the absorbance intensity of MnO2 nanosheets solution at 376nm was monitored as indicator of the concentration of GSH. As can be seen from Fig. 3B, a good linear relationship between (A0 −A)/A0 and GSH concentration is obtained in a range of 0.5–10μM with a correlation coefficient square of 0.9924, where A0 and A is the absorbance signal in the absence and presence of GSH, respectively. The calibration plot shows that the limit of detection (LOD, signal-to-noise ratio of S/N=3) of GSH can be as low as 0.1μM, which indicates that this sensing procedure can satisfy the clinical and medicinal requirements about sensitive GSH detection. Meanwhile, solution of MnO2 nanosheets in the absence and presence of various amounts of GSH was recorded by a digital camera (Fig. 3C). With the increasing of GSH concentration from 0μM to 150μM, the color of MnO2 nanosheets solution is faded from yellowish-brown to colorless, which can be obviously visualized by bare eyes. In addition, we further compared the performance of this sensing approach with other reported GSH sensors, which is summarized in Table S1. The sensitivity of this MnO2 nanosheets-based colorimetric method is higher or comparable than previous reports and the detection time is much shorter than many of them. Furthermore, our method does not need any noble/heavy metals and only requires the attendance of MnO2 nanosheets, which is low cost, easy to prepare and environmentally friendly. Therefore, this assay has a great potential to be used for GSH detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of this MnO2 nanosheets-based GSH assay
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity of this sensor towards GSH, some interference including electrolytes, small biomolecules and amino acids were tested under optimized conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 , even with a high concentration, those electrolytes have little effect on the change of absorption signal and most of amino acids do not cause obvious absorption changes. It can be clearly seen that GSH possesses strikingly larger (A0 −A)/A0 value, which reveals that this detection method has a good selectivity performance towards GSH. During the studies, we noticed that other small thiol-containing molecules like cysteine (Cys) and homocysteine (HCys) also led to absorbance change, which could be an interferential factor in GSH quantifying process. However, Cys and HCys stay much lower concentration in biological systems than that of GSH and so their existence has little influence upon the detection of GSH. Hence, this sensing platform based on MnO2 nanosheets can be applied as a selective nanosensor for GSH determination without significant interference.

The good sensitivity, simplicity and selectivity of our method suggest that it might be directly applied for GSH determination in real samples. In order to assess the practicability of this GSH nanosensor, the detection of GSH in human serum samples was also executed. The experimental procedure was the same as that in the ultrapure water. Different amount of GSH was added into 0.5% human serum. After that, the samples were tested by our developed method and the commercial GSH assay kit for verification. Table 1 shows the results and all the data was obtained according to three duplicated measurements. It can be clearly seen that our method has a good consistence with the commercial GSH assay kit. Meanwhile, the recoveries are in the range of 91.4–108.3% with RSD less than 5.6%, indicating the feasibility and reliability of the developed method for GSH detection in biological fluids.

In conclusion, this manuscript describes a new type of simple, label-free, highly sensitive and selective colorimetric assay for GSH detection using MnO2 nanosheets. This assay is based on the rapid decomposition of MnO2 nanosheets in the presence of GSH, which results in a remarkable yellowish-brown color fading and an obvious decrease of the absorbance at 376nm. This colorimetric method offers several distinct advantages: (1) the developed assay does not require any complex labeling procedure; (2) the color change of the reaction system can be obviously observed by bare eyes and UV–vis spectroscopy, respectively; (3) the reaction is very fast and the whole assay can be accomplished in 5min; (4) the preparation of MnO2 nanosheets is easy to perform and the cost of them is very low. This assay offers wide detection range for GSH from 0.5 to 100μM and has a good linear relationship from 0.5μM to 10μM with a low detection limit of 0.1μM. Furthermore, this present method was used for GSH detection in human serum with high simplicity, feasibility and reproducibility, illustrating its great potential in biological sample analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21205108, 21505122, J1210060), the Startup Research Fund of Zhengzhou University (1511316004) and the Outstanding Young Talent Research Fund of Zhengzhou University (1421316038, 1521316003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.11.066.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhongming Huang received his B.A. degree from Henan Agriculture University in 2013. He has been studying for his Master degree in College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering at Zhengzhou University since 2014, where his main research interests are in nanomaterial-based sensors.

Qiyong Cai received his B.A. degree from Zhengzhou University in 2013. He has been studying for his Master degree in College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering at Zhengzhou University since 2013, where his main research interests are in nanomaterial-based sensors.
Decai Ding is an undergraduate student in College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering at Zhengzhou University, where his main research interests are in nanomaterial-based sensors.

Jia Ge obtained his Ph.D. degree in Chemistry from Hunan University in 2014. At present, he is an associate professor at Zhengzhou University. His research interests mainly focus on optical chemo/bio sensors.
Yalei Hu is a graduate student in College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering at Zhengzhou University, where his main research interests are in nanomaterial synthesis.
Jie Yang is an undergraduate student in College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering at Zhengzhou University, where her main research interests are in nanomaterial-based sensors.

Lin Zhang got her Ph.D. degree in Material Science and Engineering from Hunan University in 2007. At present, she is a faculty at Zhengzhou University. Her research interests mainly focus on nanomaterial synthesis and investigation.

Zhaohui Li received his Ph.D. degree in State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Bio Sensing and Chemometrics at Hunan University in 2007. He did his postdoctoral training in Walt Group at Tufts University from 2007 to 2009 and in Pacific Northwest National Laboratory from 2009 to 2011. At present, he is a full professor at Zhengzhou University. His research interests mainly focus on nanomaterial preparation and their applications in Chemo/Bio sensing.
